title: Trees
description: TreeNode struct with pointers, DFS traversals, BFS with array queue, and tree depth — essential for the most common interview data structure.

sections.title: TreeNode struct
sections.explanation: The standard binary tree node in C uses a struct with an int value and two pointers. Use **malloc()** to allocate nodes on the heap. Always set children to NULL initially. Free memory when done (though in interviews this is often skipped).

exercise.prompt:
Build a tree:       1
                   / \
                  2   3
                 / \
                4   5
Print the root value, left child, right child, and left-left grandchild.

validation.message: Use typedef struct with malloc allocation and pointer navigation

exercise.hint: Build nodes with newNode(). Access children with ->left and ->right.

sections.title: DFS traversals (inorder, preorder, postorder)
sections.explanation: DFS traversals visit nodes recursively. **Inorder** (left, root, right) gives sorted order for BSTs. **Preorder** (root, left, right) is useful for serialization. **Postorder** (left, right, root) for deletion. Always check `if (node == NULL) return` as the base case.

exercise.prompt: Build the tree [1,2,3,4,5] and perform an inorder traversal, printing values space-separated. Then do a preorder traversal.

validation.message: Inorder visits left-root-right, preorder visits root-left-right

exercise.hint: Inorder: left, print, right. Preorder: print, left, right. Both check NULL as base case.

sections.title: BFS with array queue
sections.explanation: Level-order traversal (BFS) uses a queue. In C, implement with an array of TreeNode pointers. Enqueue the root, then loop: dequeue, process, enqueue children. This visits nodes level by level.

exercise.prompt: Perform a BFS (level-order) traversal on tree [1,2,3,4,5]. Print all values space-separated.

validation.message: BFS visits level by level: 1, then 2 3, then 4 5
Use array queue with front/back pointers for BFS traversal

exercise.hint: Enqueue root, loop while front < back, dequeue and enqueue children. Level order: 1 2 3 4 5.

sections.title: Tree depth
sections.explanation: The **depth** (or height) of a tree is the longest path from root to leaf. Compute recursively: `1 + max(depth(left), depth(right))`. Base case: NULL node has depth 0. This is one of the most common tree interview questions.

exercise.prompt: Compute and print the maximum depth of tree [1,2,3,4,5]. The tree has 3 levels so depth is 3.

validation.message: Tree has 3 levels: [1] -> [2,3] -> [4,5], so depth is 3
Recursively compute max of left and right depth plus 1

exercise.hint: NULL returns 0. Otherwise return 1 + max(left_depth, right_depth). Use ternary for max.
